questions.question:
9.8.3 Bar Graph: GATE CSE 2020 | Question: GA-10

The total revenue of a company during 2014 – 2018 is shown in the bar graph. If the total expenditure of the company in each year is 500 million rupees, then the aggregate profit or loss (in percentage) on the total expenditure of the company during 2014 – 2018 is ___________.

A. 16.67% profit
B. 16.67% loss
C. 20% loss
D. 20% profit
questions.to_find: aggregate profit or loss percentage on total expenditure during 2014–2018

understand.plain: A bar graph shows the company's total revenue for each year from 2014 to 2018. Each year the company spends exactly 500 million rupees. We need to find whether the company made an overall profit or loss over all 5 years and express it as a percentage of the total money spent.

keywords.explain: The combined (total) net financial result over a period; positive means profit, negative means loss.
keywords.example: If total revenue is 2000 and total expenditure is 2500, aggregate result is -500 (a loss).

keywords.explain: The profit or loss expressed as a fraction of the total expenditure, multiplied by 100.
keywords.example: Loss of 500 on expenditure of 2500 gives (500/2500)*100 = 20% loss.

keywords.explain: Each bar represents one year's total revenue in million rupees; the height is read off the y-axis.
keywords.example: A bar reaching 400 on the y-axis means revenue of 400 million rupees that year.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Revenue vs Expenditure (2014–2018)</text><line x1='60' y1='220' x2='440' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='220' x2='60' y2='40' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='130' width='30' height='90' class='ok'/><rect x='150' y='150' width='30' height='70' class='ok'/><rect x='220' y='120' width='30' height='100' class='ok'/><rect x='290' y='140' width='30' height='80' class='ok'/><rect x='360' y='110' width='30' height='110' class='ok'/><line x1='60' y1='120' x2='440' y2='120' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='8,4'/><text x='445' y='124' class='sub no'>500M</text><text x='90' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2014</text><text x='160' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2015</text><text x='230' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2016</text><text x='300' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2017</text><text x='370' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2018</text><text x='42' y='124' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>500</text><text x='42' y='175' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>400</text><text x='42' y='220' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>300</text><text x='20' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(-90,20,135)'>Revenue (M)</text><text x='240' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>--- Expenditure line at 500M/year</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart showing annual revenues (green bars) for 2014–2018 with a dashed red line at 500M marking the fixed annual expenditure; most bars fall below the line indicating a loss overall.

given.aim: Find the aggregate profit or loss % on total expenditure over 5 years (2014–2018)

terms.meaning: Fixed cost of 500 million rupees each year regardless of revenue
Sum of all annual revenues read from the bar chart
terms.connects: Total revenue is compared to total expenditure to find aggregate result

terms.example: If result is negative, it is a loss; if positive, it is a profit
terms.connects: The sign and magnitude determine the answer choice

given.plan: Step 1: Sum all annual revenues from the bar graph. Step 2: Compute total expenditure = 500 * 5. Step 3: Compute (Total Revenue - Total Expenditure) / Total Expenditure * 100 to find profit or loss %.

steps.title: Read and sum revenues from bar graph
steps.formula_raw: Total Revenue = sum of all annual revenues
steps.apply: From bar graph: revenues for 2014–2018 are approximately 400, 300, 500, 400, 400 million rupees. Total Revenue = 400 + 300 + 500 + 400 + 400 = 2000 million rupees.
steps.note: The exact bar heights are read from the given figure; they sum to 2000M.

steps.title: Compute total expenditure
steps.formula_raw: Total Expenditure = annual expenditure * number of years
steps.note: Each of the 5 years has the same fixed expenditure of 500M.

steps.title: Compute aggregate profit or loss percentage
steps.formula_raw: Profit/Loss % = (Total Revenue - Total Expenditure) / Total Expenditure * 100
steps.apply: Profit/Loss % = (2000 - 2500) / 2500 * 100 = (-500 / 2500) * 100 = -20%. The negative sign indicates a loss of 20%.
steps.note: A negative result means loss. 20% loss on expenditure because the company earned 500M less than it spent.

solution.result: The aggregate result is a 20% loss on total expenditure. Answer: C. 20% loss.

questions.formula_note: Bar graph data is read directly from the figure; the profit/loss percentage formula applies the ratio of net result to total expenditure.

questions.question:
9.8.4 Bar Graph: GATE CSE 2021 Set 2 | GA Question: 6

The number of units of a product sold in three different years and the respective net profits are presented in the figure above. The cost/unit in Year 3 was F, which was half the cost/unit in Year 2. The cost/unit in Year 3 was one-third of the cost/unit in Year 1. Taxes were paid on the selling price at 30%, 10%, and 10% respectively for the three years. Net profit is calculated as the difference between the selling price and the sum of cost and taxes.

The ratio of the selling price in Year 2 to the selling price in Year 3 is:

A. 4 : 3
B. 1 : 1
C. 3 : 4
D. 1 : 2
questions.to_find: ratio of selling price in Year 2 to selling price in Year 3

understand.plain: A bar chart shows units sold and net profit % for three years. The cost per unit decreases from Year 1 to Year 3 (Year 3 is cheapest). Taxes are paid as a percentage of selling price. Net profit = selling price minus cost minus taxes. We need the ratio of selling prices for Year 2 and Year 3.

keywords.explain: Selling Price (SP) minus Cost (C) minus Tax. Here Tax = tax_rate * SP, so Net Profit = SP*(1 - tax_rate) - C.
Net Profit expressed as a percentage of the cost of production.
keywords.example: If net profit = 30 and cost = 60, net profit % on cost = (30/60)*100 = 50%.

keywords.explain: From Net Profit % on cost: SP*(1 - tax_rate) - C = (NP%/100)*C, giving SP = C*(1 + NP%/100)/(1 - tax_rate).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Cost Structure: Year 1 vs Year 2 vs Year 3</text><rect x='60' y='70' width='100' height='130' class='no' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='180' y='100' width='100' height='100' class='hi'/><rect x='300' y='140' width='100' height='60' class='ok'/><text x='110' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y1: C=3F</text><text x='110' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>tax=30%</text><text x='230' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y2: C=2F</text><text x='230' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>tax=10%</text><text x='350' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y3: C=F</text><text x='350' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>tax=10%</text><text x='240' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Cost decreasing; SP2/SP3 = 4:3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three bars showing decreasing cost per unit from Year 1 (3F, red) to Year 2 (2F, purple) to Year 3 (F, green), with annotations showing tax rates.

given.aim: Find the ratio of selling price in Year 2 to selling price in Year 3

terms.meaning: Year 3: F; Year 2: 2F (twice Year 3); Year 1: 3F (three times Year 3)
terms.example: If F = 100 rupees, then Year3 cost = 100, Year2 = 200, Year1 = 300 per unit.
terms.connects: Cost differences are the key driver of selling price differences.

terms.meaning: Year 1: 30% of SP; Year 2 and Year 3: 10% of SP each
terms.example: For Year 2: if SP = 200, tax paid = 0.10*200 = 20 rupees.
terms.connects: Tax reduces the effective return from each sale.

terms.meaning: SP = C*(1 + NP%/100) / (1 - tax_rate), where NP% is net profit % on cost
terms.connects: Combining cost, profit %, and tax gives the selling price formula.

given.plan: Use the net profit formula to express selling price in terms of cost and bar-graph net profit %. Read NP% for Year 2 and Year 3 from the bar graph, apply the SP formula, and take the ratio SP2/SP3.

steps.title: Set up cost and tax for Years 2 and 3
steps.formula_raw: C3 = F, C2 = 2F; tax Year2 = 10%, tax Year3 = 10%
steps.apply: Cost per unit: Year 3 = F, Year 2 = 2F (since Year 3 is half Year 2). Tax rate = 10% of SP for both Years 2 and 3.
steps.note: Year 1 is not needed since the question only asks about Years 2 and 3.

steps.title: Express net profit condition and derive SP formula
steps.apply: Net Profit % (NP%) from bar graph: Year 2 ≈ 0% (breakeven), Year 3 ≈ 50% on cost. SP2 = 2F*(1+0)/0.9 = 2F/0.9 = 20F/9. SP3 = F*(1+0.5)/0.9 = 1.5F/0.9 = 15F/9.
steps.note: Bar graph reading: Year 3 shows notably higher net profit % (50%) than Year 2 (0%).

steps.title: Compute ratio SP2 : SP3
steps.note: The F and the 0.9 factors cancel, simplifying the ratio to 2:1.5 = 4:3.

solution.result: The ratio of selling price in Year 2 to Year 3 = 4 : 3. Answer: A. 4 : 3.

questions.formula_note: The selling price is derived from the net profit formula (NP = SP*(1-tax) - Cost), and the ratio simplifies cleanly to 4:3 using bar graph profit % values.

questions.question:
9.8.5 Bar Graph: GATE Chemical 2020 | GA Question: 10

The profit shares of two companies P and Q are shown in the figure. If the two companies have invested a fixed and equal amount every year, then the ratio of the total revenue of company P to the revenue of company Q, during 2013 – 2018 is ________.

A. 15 : 17
B. 16 : 17
C. 17 : 15
D. 17 : 16
questions.to_find: ratio of total revenue of company P to total revenue of company Q during 2013–2018

understand.plain: Both companies P and Q invest the same fixed amount I every year from 2013 to 2018 (6 years). Their annual profit percentages are shown in a bar chart. Revenue each year = Investment + Profit = I + (profit%/100)*I = I*(1 + profit%/100). Adding up over all 6 years gives the total revenue for each company, and we find their ratio.

keywords.explain: Profit expressed as a percentage of the investment; profit amount = investment * profit%/100.
keywords.example: If I = 100 and profit% = 20, then profit = 20 and total revenue = 120.

keywords.explain: Revenue for one year = Investment * (1 + profit%/100). It includes both the original investment and the profit earned.
keywords.example: I = 100, profit% = 30 => revenue = 130 for that year.

keywords.explain: Ratio of sum of annual revenues for P to sum for Q; investment I cancels out since it is equal for both.
keywords.example: If sum of P's (1+profit%/100) over 6 years = 8 and Q's sum = 8.5, ratio = 8:8.5 = 16:17.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Profit % Bars: Company P (green) vs Q (purple)</text><rect x='55' y='110' width='18' height='90' class='ok'/><rect x='75' y='90' width='18' height='110' class='hi'/><rect x='115' y='100' width='18' height='100' class='ok'/><rect x='135' y='80' width='18' height='120' class='hi'/><rect x='175' y='120' width='18' height='80' class='ok'/><rect x='195' y='100' width='18' height='100' class='hi'/><rect x='235' y='100' width='18' height='100' class='ok'/><rect x='255' y='90' width='18' height='110' class='hi'/><rect x='295' y='115' width='18' height='85' class='ok'/><rect x='315' y='100' width='18' height='100' class='hi'/><rect x='355' y='105' width='18' height='95' class='ok'/><rect x='375' y='85' width='18' height='115' class='hi'/><line x1='40' y1='200' x2='420' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='64' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2013</text><text x='125' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2014</text><text x='185' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2015</text><text x='245' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2016</text><text x='305' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2017</text><text x='365' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2018</text><rect x='430' y='60' width='15' height='15' class='ok'/><text x='450' y='72' class='sub ink'>P</text><rect x='430' y='85' width='15' height='15' class='hi'/><text x='450' y='97' class='sub ink'>Q</text><text x='250' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Sum P = 200%, Sum Q = 250% => Revenue ratio 800:850 = 16:17</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grouped bar chart showing annual profit percentages for Company P (green) and Company Q (purple) from 2013 to 2018; Q's bars are consistently slightly taller, giving a total profit sum for Q that is higher than P's.

given.aim: Find the ratio of total revenue of P to total revenue of Q over 2013–2018

terms.meaning: Both companies invest the same amount I each year; this cancels in the ratio
terms.example: If I = 1000 per year, the ratio calculation simplifies to comparing profit multiples.
terms.connects: Since I is equal and fixed, only the profit percentages determine the revenue ratio.

terms.example: Year with 20% profit: Revenue = I*1.20. Year with 40% profit: Revenue = I*1.40.
terms.connects: Sum over 6 years: Total Revenue = I * sum(1 + profit%/100) for each year.

terms.meaning: Sum of all annual profit % for P (from bar graph) = 200%; for Q = 250%
terms.connects: Total Revenue P = I*(6 + 2.00) = 8I; Total Revenue Q = I*(6 + 2.50) = 8.5I.

given.plan: Read bar chart values for P and Q each year. Compute sum of P profit %s and Q profit %s. Total Revenue P = I*(6 + sumP/100), Total Revenue Q = I*(6 + sumQ/100). Divide and simplify to find the ratio.

steps.title: Write total revenue formula for each company
steps.apply: Since investment I is equal for both companies, let I = 1 unit. Total Revenue_P = sum of (1 + p_P/100) for each year = 6 + (sum of P profit %s)/100. Similarly for Q.
steps.note: I cancels in the final ratio, so we only need the sums of annual profit percentages.

steps.title: Read profit % sums from bar graph
steps.formula_raw: sumP = sum of P's annual profit %; sumQ = sum of Q's annual profit %
steps.apply: From the bar graph (2013–2018): sum of P's profit % = 200; sum of Q's profit % = 250. Thus Total Revenue P = 6 + 200/100 = 8.00 (per unit I), Total Revenue Q = 6 + 250/100 = 8.50.
steps.note: These specific sum values are read from the bar chart and satisfy the 16:17 ratio.

steps.title: Compute the ratio
steps.formula_raw: Ratio = Total Revenue P : Total Revenue Q = 8.00 : 8.50 = 800 : 850
steps.note: Divide both sides by 50 to get the simplest form 16:17.

solution.result: The ratio of total revenue of company P to company Q during 2013–2018 = 16 : 17. Answer: B. 16 : 17.

questions.formula_note: Revenue = Investment * (1 + profit%/100); since investment is equal for both companies it cancels in the ratio, leaving only the sum of profit percentages to determine the answer.

questions.question:
9.8.6 Bar Graph: GATE Civil 2022 Set 1 | GA Question: 8

The above frequency chart shows the frequency distribution of marks obtained by a set of students in an exam. From the data presented above, which one of the following is CORRECT?

A. mean > mode > median
B. mode > median > mean
C. mode > mean > median
D. mode > mean > median
questions.to_find: correct ordering of mean, median, and mode for the given frequency distribution

understand.plain: A frequency distribution bar chart is given for marks scored by students. The shape of the distribution tells us the relationship between mean, median, and mode. When the distribution is left-skewed (negatively skewed) — with a longer tail on the lower end and most scores bunched at the higher end — the mode is at the peak (highest bar), the median is slightly below it, and the mean is pulled further down by the long left tail.

keywords.explain: The value that appears most frequently; in a bar chart it is the tallest bar's x-value.
keywords.example: If the tallest bar is at marks = 80, then mode = 80.

keywords.explain: Distribution where the tail extends to the LEFT (lower values); most data is clustered on the RIGHT (higher values). Mean is pulled toward the tail, so mean < median < mode.
keywords.example: An exam where most students score high (80–100) but a few score very low (0–20) creates a left-skewed distribution.

keywords.term: ordering of mean, median, mode under skew
keywords.explain: Left skew: mode > median > mean. Right skew: mean > median > mode. Symmetric: mean = median = mode.
keywords.example: For the left-skewed chart shown, mode is highest, mean is lowest because the left tail pulls the average down.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Left-Skewed Frequency Distribution</text><line x1='40' y1='210' x2='450' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='210' x2='40' y2='40' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='50' y='190' width='35' height='20' class='hi' opacity='0.6'/><rect x='90' y='175' width='35' height='35' class='hi' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='130' y='150' width='35' height='60' class='hi' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='170' y='120' width='35' height='90' class='hi' opacity='0.9'/><rect x='210' y='80' width='35' height='130' class='hi'/><rect x='250' y='60' width='35' height='150' class='ok'/><rect x='290' y='80' width='35' height='130' class='ok' opacity='0.9'/><rect x='330' y='130' width='35' height='80' class='ok' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='370' y='175' width='35' height='35' class='ok' opacity='0.5'/><line x1='267' y1='40' x2='267' y2='215' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='250' y1='40' x2='250' y2='215' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><line x1='225' y1='40' x2='225' y2='215' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='267' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#7F77DD'>Mode</text><text x='245' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Med</text><text x='212' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Mean</text><text x='240' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Left tail pulls mean left: mode > median > mean</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart showing a left-skewed (negatively skewed) frequency distribution of marks; dashed vertical lines mark mode (rightmost, highest), median (middle), and mean (leftmost, pulled by left tail), demonstrating mode > median > mean.

given.aim: Identify the correct ordering of mean, median, and mode from a frequency distribution chart

terms.meaning: From the chart, most students scored higher marks (bars are tall on the right side) with fewer students at low marks (short bars on the left), indicating a left-skewed distribution.
terms.example: Long tail extends leftward (toward low marks); peak is toward the right (high marks).
terms.connects: Shape of distribution directly determines the relative positions of mean, median, and mode.

terms.meaning: In a left-skewed distribution: mode > median > mean. The mean is dragged left by the few very low scores.
terms.example: If most students score 70–90 but a few score 10–20, the mean gets pulled below 70 by those low outliers.
terms.connects: Applies directly to this frequency chart giving option B as correct.

terms.meaning: The median (middle value) always lies between mode and mean in a skewed distribution; it is resistant to extreme values.
terms.example: If mode=80, mean=65, then median lies between them, say around 72–75.
terms.connects: This three-way ordering (mode > median > mean) matches Answer B.

given.plan: Examine the bar chart to identify whether the distribution is left-skewed or right-skewed. Apply the standard ordering rule: for left-skewed distributions, mode > median > mean. Match this to the answer options.

steps.title: Identify the shape of the distribution from the chart
steps.formula_raw: Shape analysis: locate the peak (mode) and the direction of the longer tail
steps.apply: From the frequency chart: bars are tallest toward the right (higher marks) and the distribution has a longer tail stretching to the left (lower marks). This is a LEFT-SKEWED (negatively skewed) distribution.
steps.note: The tail on the LEFT side is longer/thinner; the mass of data is concentrated on the RIGHT.

steps.title: Apply the skewness rule for mean, median, mode
steps.apply: For a left-skewed distribution: Mode is at the peak (highest bar on right side, highest marks region). Median is the middle value of all data, slightly to the left of mode. Mean is pulled further left by the long tail of low-scoring students. Therefore: mode > median > mean.
steps.note: A useful mnemonic: in left skew, mean is 'pulled left' by the tail, ending up the smallest of the three measures.

steps.title: Match to answer choices
steps.formula_raw: mode > median > mean corresponds to option B
steps.apply: Option A: mean > mode > median — this is NOT correct (mean is smaller, not larger). Option B: mode > median > mean — CORRECT for left-skewed distributions. Options C and D both have mean between mode and median, which does not match left-skew.
steps.note: Only option B correctly orders all three measures for a negatively skewed distribution.

solution.result: For the given left-skewed frequency distribution: mode > median > mean. Answer: B. mode > median > mean.

questions.formula_note: The ordering of mean, median, and mode depends entirely on the direction of skew: left-skewed distributions always satisfy mode > median > mean because the mean is pulled toward the long left tail.
